288	\begin{definition}\leanok
289	[Push--pull object map]
290	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
292	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
293	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
294	  \[
295	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
296	  \]
297	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
298	\end{definition}
299	
300	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
301	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
303	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
304	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
305	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
306	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
307	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
308	  \[
309	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
310	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
311	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
312	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
313	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
314	      \;=\;
315	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
316	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
317	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
318	  \]
319	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
320	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
321	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
322	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
323	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
324	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
325	\end{definition}
326	
327	\begin{lemma}
328	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
329	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
330	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
331	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
332	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
333	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
334	\end{lemma}
335	
336	\begin{proof}
337	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
338	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
339	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
340	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
341	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
342	\end{proof}
343	
344	\begin{lemma}
345	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
346	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
348	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
349	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
350	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
351	  \[
352	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
353	  \]
354	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
355	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
356	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
357	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
358	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
359	\end{lemma}
360	
361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
363	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
364	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
365	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
366	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
367	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
368	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
369	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
370	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\begin{lemma}
374	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
375	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
377	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
378	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
379	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
380	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
381	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
382	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
383	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
384	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
385	  \[
386	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
387	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
388	    \;=\;
389	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
390	  \]
391	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
392	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
393	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
394	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
399	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
400	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
401	\end{proof}
402	
403	\begin{lemma}
404	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
405	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
407	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
408	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
409	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
410	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
411	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
412	  collapses to the transport-light form
413	  \[
414	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
415	      \;\circ\; e_2
416	    \;=\;
417	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
418	      \;\circ\; e_3,
419	  \]
420	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
421	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
422	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
423	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
424	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
425	  composition (pentagon) law.
426	\end{lemma}
427	
428	\begin{proof}
429	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
430	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
431	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
432	\end{proof}
433	
434	\begin{definition}
435	[Push--pull functor]
436	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
437	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
438	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
439	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
440	  \[
441	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
442	  \]
443	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
444	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
445	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
446	  laws established above: the identity law
447	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
448	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
449	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
450	\end{definition}

452	\begin{definition}
453	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
454	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
456	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
457	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
458	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
459	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
460	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
461	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
462	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
463	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
464	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
465	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
466	\end{definition}

468	\begin{definition}\leanok
469	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
470	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
472	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
473	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
474	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
475	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
476	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
477	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
478	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
479	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
480	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
481	\end{definition}

483	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
484	
485	\begin{definition}
486	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
487	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
489	  \mathlibok
490	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
491	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
492	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
493	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
494	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
495	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
496	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
497	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
498	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
499	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
500	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
501	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
502	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
503	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
504	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
505	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
506	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
507	\end{definition}
508	
509	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
510	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
511	
512	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
513	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
514	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
515	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
516	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
517	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
518	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
519	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
520	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
521	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
522	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
523	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
524	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
525	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
526	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
527	
528	\begin{definition}
529	
530	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
531	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
533	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
534	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
535	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
536	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
537	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
538	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
539	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
540	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
541	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
542	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
543	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
544	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
545	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
546	  % as an $R_f$-module.
547	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
548	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
549	  % are the maps
550	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
551	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
552	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
553	  items (4)--(5).}
554	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
555	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
556	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
557	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
558	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
559	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
560	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
561	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
562	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
563	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
564	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
565	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
566	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
567	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
568	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
569	
570	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
571	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
572	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
573	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
574	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
575	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
576	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
577	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
578	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
579	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
580	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
581	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
582	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
583	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
584	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
585	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
586	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
587	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
588	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
589	
590	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
591	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
592	  intersection identity
593	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
594	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
595	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
596	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
597	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
598	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
599	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
600	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
601	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
602	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
603	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
604	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
605	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
606	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
607	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
608	
609	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
610	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
611	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
612	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
613	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
614	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
615	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
616	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
617	  restriction map through these comparisons.
618	\end{definition}

656	  % $$
657	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
658	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
659	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
660	  % \ldots
661	  % $$"
662	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
663	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
664	  complex of localisations).}
665	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
666	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
667	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
668	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
669	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
670	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
671	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
672	  \[
673	    D^\bullet : \quad
674	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
675	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
676	    \cdots,
677	    \qquad
678	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
679	  \]
680	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
681	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
682	  \[
683	    1 \leq p
684	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
685	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
686	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
687	  \]
688	  which is the form the affine vanishing
689	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
690	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
691	  homomorphisms,
692	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
693	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
694	
695	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
696	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
697	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
698	  product}
699	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
700	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
701	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
702	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
703	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
704	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
705	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
706	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
707	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
708	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
709	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
710	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
711	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
712	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
713	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
714	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
715	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
716	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
717	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
718	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
719	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
720	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
721	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
722	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
723	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
724	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
725	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
726	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
727	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
728	\end{lemma}
729	
730	\begin{proof}
731	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
732	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
733	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
734	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
735	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
736	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
737	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
738	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
739	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
740	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
741	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
742	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
743	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
744	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
745	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
746	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
747	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
748	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
749	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
750	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
751	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
752	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
753	  element-level product equivalence
754	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
755	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
756	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
757	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
758	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
759	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
760	
761	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
762	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
763	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
764	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
765	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
766	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
767	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
768	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
769	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
770	  being proved.
771	\end{proof}

775	[Element-level product equivalence for the section {\v C}ech terms]
776	  \label{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
777	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv,
778	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv_apply,
779	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdAddEquiv}
780	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
781	  Let \(\{F_\sigma\}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}\) be the finite family of
782	  abelian groups \(F_\sigma = \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) appearing in degree \(p\) of
783	  the section {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}. There is
784	  an equivalence of underlying types
785	  \[
786	    \operatorname{ToType}\Bigl(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\Bigr)
787	    \;\simeq\;
788	    \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma),
789	  \]
790	  between the elements of the categorical product \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\) in
791	  \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the dependent functions \(\sigma \mapsto\) (element of
792	  \(F_\sigma\)). It is supplied by the Mathlib concrete-limit comparison
793	  \(\operatorname{CategoryTheory.Limits.Concrete.productEquiv}\), which holds because the
794	  forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete
795	  product (\(\operatorname{PreservesLimit}(\operatorname{Discrete.functor} F)\)). Under
796	  this equivalence each coordinate
797	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)))\) is identified with the away
798	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{dCoeff}\) of
799	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} by the
800	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) uniqueness of
801	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} (both
802	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and \(M_{s_\sigma}\) localise \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\), so
803	  the comparison is the canonical isomorphism). This is the move between the
804	  categorical product of \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the per-\(\sigma\) localised modules.
805	  Its packaging as an additive isomorphism --- the degreewise vertical map of the
806	  ladder transport in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact} --- is recorded as
807	  \(\operatorname{sectionProdAddEquiv}\), the \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) underlying
808	  the coordinatewise comparison.
809	  The coordinate projection of the equivalence is recorded separately as the
810	  definitional restatement
811	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\): the \(\sigma\)-component of the
812	  image of \(y\) under the equivalence is the underlying group map of the categorical
813	  projection \(\pi_\sigma\) applied to \(y\).
814	\end{lemma}
815	
816	\begin{proof}
817	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
818	  The type-level equivalence is \(\operatorname{Concrete.productEquiv}\) applied to the
819	  family \(\sigma \mapsto F_\sigma\); its hypothesis is that
820	  \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete-diagram limit, which is
821	  the standard instance for \(\mathrm{Ab}\). The per-coordinate identification
822	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))) \cong M_{s_\sigma}\) is the
823	  uniqueness clause of \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\): by
824	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} the restriction map
825	  \(M \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and the localisation unit
826	  \(M \to M_{s_\sigma}\) are both localisations of \(M\) at
827	  \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\)
828	  (\(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\)), hence canonically isomorphic over
829	  \(M\). Assembling the coordinatewise isomorphisms after the product equivalence gives
830	  the stated identification.
831	\end{proof}

842	  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
843	  \[
844	    \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) :
845	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)
846	      \;\longrightarrow\;
847	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr)
848	  \]
849	  for the \(i\)-th presheaf face restriction --- the value of \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
850	  inclusion of opens
851	  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)
852	  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
853	  read through the product equivalence
854	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\) of
855	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
856	  degree-\(q\) differential
857	  \(\operatorname{objD} q\) of the alternating-coface-map complex
858	  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}
859	  (\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\,\mathcal{U}\,\mathcal{F})\) is the alternating
860	  sum of the face restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates:
861	  \[
862	    \bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q+1}\,(\operatorname{objD} q\, t)\bigr)_\sigma
863	      \;=\;
864	      \sum_{i = 0}^{q+1} (-1)^i \,
865	        \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
866	          \Bigl(\bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q}\, t\bigr)_{\sigma \circ d_i}\Bigr).
867	  \]
868	  This is \emph{purely cosimplicial}: it records the shape of the section {\v C}ech
869	  differential as an alternating sum of presheaf restrictions, with \emph{no}
870	  sheaf-theoretic or localisation identification of those restrictions. It is the
871	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
872	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge
873	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} is supplied.
874	\end{lemma}
875	
876	\begin{proof}
877	  \uses{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
878	  Unfold the degree-\(q\) map of \(\operatorname{alternatingCofaceMapComplex}\) on
879	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\): by construction \(\operatorname{objD} q\)
880	  is the alternating sum \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, d^i\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, each
881	  coface \(d^i\) being the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component
882	  projects to the coordinate \(\sigma \circ d_i\) and post-composes with the presheaf
883	  restriction \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\). Reading the
884	  \(\sigma\)-coordinate through the product equivalence (the coordinate-projection
885	  restatement \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\)) commutes the projection
886	  past the categorical sum: applying the sum of \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-morphisms to an element
887	  distributes over the sum (the finite-sum-application identity
888	  \(\operatorname{ab\_hom\_finsetSum\_apply}\)), and each summand becomes
889	  \((-1)^i\) times the face restriction of the \((\sigma \circ d_i)\)-coordinate. The
890	  per-coordinate projection of the cosimplicial coface is the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\)
891	  computation \(\operatorname{Pi.lift\_π}\), which identifies the projected coface with
892	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) precomposed with the projection to
893	  \(\sigma \circ d_i\). Assembling the signs yields the stated alternating sum.
894	\end{proof}

33	### Route A — acyclic-resolution comparison (CHOSEN)
34	The augmented Čech complex `0 → F → C⁰ → C¹ → ⋯` on `X` (`Cᵖ = ∏ (j_s)_*(F|_{U_s})`) is
35	(i) a resolution of `F` and (ii) termwise `(pushforward f)`-acyclic. The P4 abstract lemma
36	"a `G`-acyclic resolution computes `G.rightDerived`" then gives `Hⁱ(f_* C•) ≅ Rⁱf_* F`
37	directly — ONE abstract lemma, NO spectral sequences. The standard Cartan–Leray acyclic-cover
38	existence proof. Its acyclicity input (ii) reduces to affine Serre vanishing
39	`H^q(affine, qcoh)=0`, which is NOT free: see the bridge below.

41	### Route B — two spectral sequences (REJECTED, fallback only)
42	Čech-to-derived + Leray spectral sequences for `Scheme.Modules`. Rejected: both absent from
43	Mathlib (multi-thousand-LOC), and Leray needs quasi-coherence of `R^q f_* F`. Strictly heavier
44	than Route A for the same `Nonempty (…≅…)` goal. NOTE: Route B rests on the SAME irreducible
45	brick `injective_cech_acyclic` as Route A — rejecting B does not escape it. Fallback only.

47	### The acyclicity bridge (load-bearing, CORRECTED)
48	Route A's term-/relative-acyclicity inputs and the general-cover intersection vanishing all
49	reduce to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(Spec A, qcoh)=0` (Stacks 02KG). This is NOT obtainable
50	from the P3 contracting homotopy alone: the homotopy proves the Čech *complex* is exact (a
51	resolution), but term `G`-acyclicity is itself affine vanishing on a smaller affine — a circular
52	regress with no inductive base. The honest route (Stacks, torsor-free) is the minimal
53	Čech↔derived bridge P3b: (1) injective `O_X`-modules are Čech-acyclic
54	(`injective_cech_acyclic`); (2) `ses_cech_h1`; (3) the dimension-shift `cech_vanish_basis` (01EO)
55	consuming the standard-cover Čech vanishing of P3 as its condition (3). This yields
56	`affine_serre_vanishing` legitimately, breaking the cycle: P3 produces standard-cover Čech
57	vanishing; P3b lifts it to affine sheaf vanishing without ever using affine vanishing as an input.
58	
59	**Direct route for (1)**: `injective_cech_acyclic` does NOT
60	need presheaf enough-injectives or the δ-functor universality. Two aligned parts: (a) injective
61	sheaf ⟹ injective presheaf via `Injective.injective_of_adjoint` applied to the
62	`Mod ↪ PMod` right adjoint (`sheafificationAdjunction`); (b) the free-presheaf complex
63	`K(𝒰)_• = ⨁ free(yoneda U_…)` resolves `O_𝒰` (objectwise contracting homotopy) and
64	`Hom(K_•,F) = Č•(𝒰,F)` (section complex) via `freeAdjunction`+Yoneda — so for injective `I`,
65	`Hom(-,I)` exact gives positive Čech vanishing. This BYPASSES the two expensive bricks
66	(`presheafModules_enoughInjectives`, `cech_delta_functor_presheaves` — both off the critical path,
67	dropped from the blueprint). The free-presheaf complex uses Mathlib's `PresheafOfModules.free`, NOT
68	a bespoke `j_!`. The FULL Stacks-01EO bootstrap (torsor `lemma-cech-h1`, `lemma-kill-cohomology-class`)
69	remains avoidable; only the injective-acyclicity brick is irreducible.

1289	  restriction is the canonical localisation map. Hence
1290	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} identifies the section {\v C}ech complex
1291	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1292	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} with the concrete complex of localised
1293	  \(R\)-modules
1294	  \[
1295	    \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \;\to\;
1296	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1}} \;\to\;
1297	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1} s_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
1298	  \]
1299	  and reduces the vanishing
1300	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1301	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\) for \(p > 0\) to positive-degree
1302	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying localisation maps. Equivalently,
1303	  the extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
1304	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is
1305	  exact in positive degrees. The active target is the tilde case
1306	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), where the section identification of
1307	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} is gap-free and the coface match is
1308	  the abstract unfold of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} composed with the
1309	  tilde bridge of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}; a general quasi-coherent
1310	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case by \(\mathcal{F} \cong
1311	  \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the separately-deferred
1312	  globalisation gap.
1313	
1314	  \paragraph{Node-by-node exactness via local-to-global and the dependent port.}
1315	  Exactness of each positive-degree node is established from the spanning family
1316	  \((s, hs)\). The local-to-global exactness criterion
1317	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\)
1318	  reduces \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p-1}, d^{p})\) to exactness after
1319	  localising at each spanning element \(r\) (the away localisation
1320	  \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)), checking exactness one localisation at a time. In the
1321	  localisation at \(s_r\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes \(s_r\)
1322	  invertible, so the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport \(c\)
1323	  (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) is defined globally on that localisation and
1324	  furnishes a contracting homotopy of the localised complex: the unit, shift, and
1325	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\) (\(c \circ \delta_0 =
1326	  \operatorname{id}\)), \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (\(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)),
1327	  and \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) hold for the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\). The node is
1328	  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,
1329	  which packages the identity \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) into the
1330	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that
1331	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}\) consumes. The constant- and
1332	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial cores and the spanning-family certifier are
1333	  already in place; the section and differential identifications of
1334	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and the homology--exactness reduction of
1335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} are the bridge that connects them to the
1336	  abstract section complex.
1337	
1338	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
1339	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
1340	  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
1341	  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
1342	  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
1343	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
1344	  is used instead.
1345	\end{proof}

1347	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1348	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1349	
1350	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1351	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1352	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1353	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1354	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1355	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1356	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1357	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1358	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1359	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1360	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1361	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1362	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1363	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1364	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1365	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1366	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1367	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1368	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1369	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1370	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1371	
1372	\begin{definition}
1373	
1374	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
1375	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
1377	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,
1378	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,
1379	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,
1380	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen_comp_le,
1381	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial,
1382	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex_X,
1383	    AlgebraicGeometry.sigma_ι_eqToHom_transport}
1384	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1385	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1386	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1387	  % L1095--1135).
1388	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1389	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1390	  % $$
1391	  % \ldots
1392	  % \to
1393	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1394	  % \to
1395	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1396	  % \to
1397	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1398	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1399	  % $$
1400	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1401	  % and where the map
1402	  % $$
1403	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1404	  % \longrightarrow
1405	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1406	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1407	  % $$
1408	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1409	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1410	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1411	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1412	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1413	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1414	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1415	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1416	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1417	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1418	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1419	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1420	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1421	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1422	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1423	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1424	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1425	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1426	  \[
1427	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1428	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1429	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1430	  \]
1431	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1432	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1433	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1434	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1435	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1436	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1437	  \[
1438	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1439	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1440	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1441	  \]
1442	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1443	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1444	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1445	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1446	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1447	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1448	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1449	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1450	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1451	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1452	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1453	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1454	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1455	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1456	
1457	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1458	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1459	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1460	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1461	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1462	\end{definition}

1497	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1498	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1499	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1500	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1501	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1502	  \[
1503	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1504	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1505	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1506	  \]
1507	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1508	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1509	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1510	  \[
1511	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1512	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1513	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1514	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1515	  \]
1516	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1517	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1518	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1519	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1520	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1521	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1522	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1523	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1524	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.

1595	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1596	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1597	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1598	  \[
1599	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1600	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1601	    \bigr)
1602	    \;\cong\;
1603	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1604	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1605	    \;\cong\;
1606	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1607	  \]
1608	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1609	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1610	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1611	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =

1613	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1614	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1615	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1616	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1617	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1618	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1619	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1620	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1621	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1622	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1623	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1624	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1625	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1626	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1627	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1628	\end{proof}

1653	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1654	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1655	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1656	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1657	  \[
1658	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1659	  \]
1660	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1661	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1662	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1663	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1664	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1665	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1666	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1667	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image
1668	  presheaf.
1669	\end{definition}

1671	\begin{lemma}
1672	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1673	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1674	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1675	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1676	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1677	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1678	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1679	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1680	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1681	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1682	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1683	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1684	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1685	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1686	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1687	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1688	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1689	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1690	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1691	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1692	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1693	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1694	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1695	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1696	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1697	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1698	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1699	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1700	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1701	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1702	\end{lemma}
1703	
1704	\begin{proof}
1705	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1706	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1707	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1708	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1709	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1710	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1711	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1712	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1713	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1714	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1715	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1716	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1717	  quasi-isomorphism.
1718	\end{proof}

1722	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1723	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}

1737	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1738	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1739	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1740	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1741	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1742	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1743	  \[
1744	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1745	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1746	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1747	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}

1756	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1757	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated

1760	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1761	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1762	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1763	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1764	  The Lean entry point for the degree-\(p\) unfolding is
1765	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes
1766	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p = \coprod_\sigma \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)\) by

1769	  identification is assembled from three pieces: \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\)

1777	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1778	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).

1782	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1783	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1784	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1785	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1786	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1787	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1788	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1789	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1790	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1791	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1792	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1793	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1794	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1795	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1796	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1797	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1798	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1799	\end{proof}
